title: From Football Pitch to Media Empire: The Rivalry of Lineker and Neville
summary: Gary Lineker and Gary Neville, two of England's most capped footballers, never played against each other but are now fierce rivals in the media industry. Each has built a digital empire: Lineker co-founded Goalhanger in 2014, while Neville launched The Overlap in 2021. The Overlap recently acquired the YouTube channels of popular influencer Mark Goldbridge, aiming to create a leading independent football community. The Overlap's parent company Buzz16 generated £11.6 million in revenue last year. Meanwhile, Goalhanger struck a deal with Netflix to produce 'The Rest Is Football' podcast during the World Cup, reportedly earning £14 million. Both platforms are vying for dominance in sports media, with Neville's platform boasting 2.2 billion views across all platforms in 2025. The rivalry highlights the shift from traditional broadcasting to digital content, where former players leverage their fame to control the narrative and engage fans directly.

background_read: Gary Lineker and Gary Neville are among the most recognizable faces in English football. After retiring, they moved into broadcasting and punditry. Lineker became the host of BBC's Match of the Day, while Neville became a prominent pundit on Sky Sports. Both have since expanded into media entrepreneurship, creating content that appeals to football fans. The rise of digital platforms like YouTube and podcasting has allowed them to bypass traditional media and build direct relationships with audiences. Their rivalry mirrors the broader competition in sports media, where personality-driven content is increasingly valued.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The article highlights the rivalry between Gary Lineker and Gary Neville as they build competing media empires. Key points include Neville's acquisition of Mark Goldbridge's YouTube channels, the financial performance of their companies, and their deals with major platforms like Netflix. Purpose: The article aims to inform readers about the business side of football media and how former players are leveraging their fame. Evidence Evaluation: The article uses specific financial figures (e.g., £11.6m revenue) and view counts (2.2 billion views) as evidence, though these are from the companies' own claims. Author Credibility: The article is from BBC Sport, a reputable source. Methodology: The article gathers information from company announcements and reports, presenting a comparative analysis of the two entities.
